critique.summary: The article presents a website called Ramtrack.eu that offers memory price intelligence, focusing on RAM prices for DDR4 and DDR5 types. It provides features such as price history and price alerts for different RAM capacities, although the current data appears to be loading or unavailable.
mainPoints: Ramtrack.eu provides memory price intelligence specifically for RAM.
The site tracks average prices per gigabyte for DDR4 and DDR5 RAM.
Users can view price history and set up price alerts for various RAM capacities.
Current price data and alerts appear to be loading or not configured.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the website's purpose and features are consistent with typical price tracking services for computer components. However, the article does not provide any external sources or data to verify the accuracy or comprehensiveness of the pricing information. The lack of actual price data in the article limits the ability to assess the accuracy of the price intelligence offered.
considerations: The article does not mention the data sources or methodology used to gather RAM prices, which is important for assessing reliability.
There is no information about how frequently prices are updated or the geographic scope beyond a mention of the Dutch market.
The article does not compare Ramtrack.eu to other similar services or provide user reviews or expert opinions.
The site’s current data loading issues may affect usability and reliability.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the current RAM market conditions, with some users expressing skepticism about pricing and supply issues, while others share personal experiences with RAM purchases and hardware decisions. There are suggestions for improving the RAM price tracking tool and concerns about market dynamics favoring certain manufacturers.

comments.author: sudo_gopnik
comments.text:
Nice - Recommend adding LPDDR variants, info on lead times, currency toggle button, and lastly maybe consider adding other memories commonly paired (e.g. eMMC, NVMe, etc.) but perhaps is out of scope.

This supply crunch is such a fraud - I was on a call with a analyst group covering the memory market and they described the current situation in hilariously depressing corpo speak:

"Pricing dynamics are reflective of coordinated production discipline amongst major suppliers."

I had to give them props, that is one of the most creative ways to describe the pricing fixing cartels.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a well-reasoned critique of the market situation and constructive suggestions for the tool, highlighting potential cartel-like behavior in pricing.

comments.author: vardump
comments.text:
RAM market havoc is handing Chinese manufacturers an open door. They'll generate enough cash to finally catch up with the big guys.

This is not going to end well for Samsung, Micron and Hynix.
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully points out geopolitical and market shifts affecting major RAM manufacturers, reflecting plausible industry trends.

comments.author: whalesalad
comments.text: I have 2x32GB DDR4 from Teamgroup that I purchased in 2023 for about $100. One of the sticks recently died. The RMA process has been a nightmare, so I looked on AMZN to check and see how expensive it would be to just re-order and replace them. $600. Absolutely insane tbh.
comments.analysis: A personal anecdote illustrating current high prices and poor customer service, adding real-world context though limited to one case.

comments.author: the_biot
comments.text:
A quick glance at sold DIMMs on ebay makes clear this is just nonsense. What's the source for these numbers?

This is just some vibe-coded crap, isn't it?
comments.analysis: Raises a valid question about data sources and accuracy, encouraging scrutiny of the price intelligence claims.

comments.author: nazgulsenpai
comments.text:
In June 2024, for my home gaming PC, instead of platform swapping to AM5, I decided to coast on a 5700X3D while they were on sale for ~$190 and 32 GB DDR4 3200MHZ for ~$50. Added a 9070XT last year for MSRP but don't remember the exact price.

While it was the right idea at the time (for me), I wonder if I should have upgraded while the prices were a little more "normal"...

No real point here, just complaining to the room.
comments.analysis: A reflective personal experience about timing hardware purchases, adding a consumer perspective but no market analysis.

comments.author: burnt-resistor
comments.text:
So I have an NIB sealed Corsair Vengeance 96 GiB (2x48) DDR5-5600 SO-DIMM and it's looking like $1100 USD would be a reasonable price point given comparables and there's zero supply at present.

It's pretty crazy when computer components go tulip bulbs better than gold.
comments.analysis: Highlights extreme pricing and scarcity, using a tulip bulb analogy to emphasize perceived market irrationality.

comments.author: omarqureshi
comments.text: very cool - if RDIMMs could be added, that would be swell
comments.analysis: A brief but constructive suggestion to improve the RAM tracking tool by including RDIMM types.

comments.author: Havoc
comments.text:
Hmm. Maybe I should sell half the ddr4 in my pc.

Don’t really need 64gb
comments.analysis: A personal reaction to RAM pricing or market conditions, showing consumer behavior but lacking broader insight.

comments.author: sonar_un
comments.analysis: A simple feature request for the tool to include a specific RAM module size.

comments.author: Myzel394
comments.text: I need this for hard drives
comments.analysis: Expresses interest in expanding the price intelligence tool to cover hard drives, a reasonable but brief suggestion.

comments.author: replooda
comments.analysis: A short emotional comment reflecting disappointment, but lacking substantive content.
